title: ordenaR
text:
A free, open-source tool that helps academic authors generate properly formatted author/affiliation/email/ORCID lists for paper submissions — and reformat them when journals change or affiliations shift during peer review.

Open the Author Manager   View on GitHub

title: ordenaR
text:
The problem
You finish a paper. You spend an hour collecting affiliations from twelve coauthors over email and Slack. You paste them into the Nature template. The paper is rejected. You resubmit to Elsevier — different format. By the time it goes to the next journal, three coauthors have moved institutions and one has a present address footnote. Excel sheets are flying around. Someone’s ORCID is wrong.
ordenaR replaces that ad-hoc workflow. Send each coauthor one link. They fill in their own block in their browser and email back a clean JSON entry. You drop it in. When affiliations or journals change, you re-export — no rework.


Import Excel / CSV
Already collected author info in a spreadsheet? Upload it (xlsx, xls, csv, tsv, ods). ordenaR auto-detects the columns and lets you confirm the mapping before importing.


Word (.docx) download
Download a real Word document with title, author byline (with proper superscript affiliation numbers), affiliations list, corresponding author info, and an author details table.


Send one link, get back clean JSON
The corresponding author generates an invite link. Coauthors open it, fill their details, and the tool builds a pre-addressed email back to you. No accounts, no logins, no cloud.


CRediT contributor roles
Tick the 14 official CRediT roles per author. The tool outputs a paper-ready statement and includes contributions in the Word download. CRediT columns in your spreadsheet are auto-detected on import.


30+ output formats
Generic plain text (Nature/Science, Elsevier), journal-specific plain text (PLOS, Cell, PNAS, eLife, MDPI, Wiley, Frontiers, Royal Society, Nature Communications, Nature Methods, AGU, APA, Vancouver), a deep Ecology & Evolution suite, LaTeX (elsarticle, Springer, IEEE), Quarto YAML, Word HTML, BibTeX, CSL JSON, EndNote XML, and Markdown profiles — each with one click.


Sort flexibly
Sort manually (drag & drop) or alphabetically by last name. Optional “keep senior authors at the end”.


ORCID validation
Every ORCID is checked against the official ISO 7064 MOD 11-2 checksum, so you don’t ship typos.


Privacy by design
No backend, no analytics, no tracking. All data lives in localStorage in your browser.


Mobile-friendly
Works down to 375 px width. Keyboard-navigable.

title: ordenaR
text:
How it works in 60 seconds

You open the Author Manager, enter the project title, and fill in your own (corresponding author) block.
Click Generate invitation link. Email it to each coauthor.
Each coauthor opens the link, fills their own block, and clicks Send to corresponding author — their email client opens with their JSON entry pre-filled.
You paste each reply into the Coauthor entries panel. The tool flags duplicates and shows a preview before merging.
Pick a journal format from the dropdown. Copy or download. Done.

When the paper bounces to a new journal, change the dropdown and re-export.

text:

title: About
text:
Citation
If ordenaR supports your work, please cite it:

Leiva, F. P. (2026). ordenaR: a free, open-source tool for managing author lists in academic papers (Version 1.0.0) [Software]. https://felixpleiva.github.io/ordenaR/

The repository ships a CITATION.cff file in Citation File Format v1.2.0, so GitHub will display a “Cite this repository” button at the top right of the repo page and offer BibTeX/APA conversions automatically.
A BibTeX entry:
@software{leiva_ordenaR_2026,
  author       = {Leiva, F{\'e}lix P.},
  title        = {{ordenaR}: A free, open-source tool for managing author lists in academic papers},
  year         = {2026},
  version      = {1.0.0},
  url          = {https://felixpleiva.github.io/ordenaR/},
  note         = {MIT licensed}
}

title: About
text:
License

Code — MIT License. Use it in your lab, fork it, modify it, ship it. Attribution is appreciated but not legally required.
Documentation (this site, README, etc.) — CC-BY 4.0. Re-use freely with attribution.

title: Author Manager
text: The Author Manager is a single-page application that runs entirely in your browser. Your data is saved to localStorage; nothing is sent to a server.

title: Author Manager
text:
Tips & shortcuts

Drag the ⋮⋮ handle on the left of any author card to reorder authors (only when sort mode is “Manual”).
Sort mode has two settings: Manual and Alphabetical (by last name). When you switch back to manual, the current displayed order is frozen as the new manual order.
ORCID validation is live; a green ✓ means the checksum (ISO 7064 MOD 11-2) is valid.
Affiliation deduplication is automatic: if two authors share an exact-match institution + city + country + department, they get the same superscript.
The output panel updates as you type. Try switching the format dropdown — your data is the same; only the rendering changes.
The Coauthor entries panel detects the BEGIN/END ORDENAR ENTRY markers in pasted email text, so you can paste the entire email body and it will extract the JSON.

title: FAQ
text:
It stays in your browser’s localStorage. ordenaR makes no network calls at runtime.



No. No Google Analytics, no Plausible, no fingerprinting, no error reporting.



You lose the project. Always export a project JSON backup (button in the right-hand Backup panel) and keep one copy. JSON is plain text — versionable, diffable, archivable.



No. ordenaR deliberately has no real-time collaboration. The corresponding author owns the master copy; coauthors fill their own entries via the invite link, and the corresponding consolidates. One source of truth, one merge step, no arguments.

No. ordenaR deliberately has no real-time collaboration. The corresponding author owns the master copy; coauthors fill their own entries via the invite link, and the corresponding consolidates. One source of truth, one merge step, no arguments.

title: FAQ
text:
Workflow

What if my coauthor’s email client doesn’t open?
The invitation page has a fallback: a “Email client didn’t open?” expandable panel. Open it; the same email body is shown in a textarea with a Copy to clipboard button. The coauthor pastes that into their webmail (Gmail, Outlook on the web, university roundcube, etc.) and sends manually.


What if my coauthor isn’t comfortable with the tool?
Skip the invite link. Ask them for the data in any format (email, Slack, Word doc) and enter it yourself. The tool’s value to you is the same.


How do I handle co-first authors?
On each co-first author’s card, tick Equal contribution and pick a group symbol (e.g. *). Use the same symbol for everyone in the group. To alphabetize them automatically, switch the sort mode to Alphabetical.


Can I have multiple equal-contribution groups (e.g. co-first AND co-last)?
Yes. Use one symbol (*) for the co-first group and a different symbol (†) for the co-last group. The output formats handle each group independently.

title: FAQ
text:
Versioning across paper revisions

My paper went through revisions and authors changed something. Do I have to ask everyone again?
No. Three possibilities, in order of effort:

You know the change (typo, new email, reordered authors) → just edit the field directly in the app and re-export.
One coauthor changed their info (new affiliation, new ORCID, redistributed CRediT) → click ✉ Invite via email on their card. The email is pre-filled with their current data inside the === ORDENAR ENTRY === markers. They edit only what changed and reply. You paste the reply into Paste invitation reply — ordenaR matches them by email and updates the card.
Many changes / many new authors → maintain a master Excel/CSV. When something changes, update the sheet and re-import via Import from Excel/CSV. Existing authors are matched by email and updated; new rows are added.

The email field is the primary key for re-imports — keep emails stable across revisions.


Should I save a JSON backup at every revision stage?
Yes — strongly recommended. Click Export project (.json) every time you reach a stable milestone:

Initial submission
After each round of revisions
Acceptance
Final published version

Name the files like ordenaR-mypaper-v1-submission.json, ordenaR-mypaper-v2-revision1.json, etc., and keep them in the same folder as your manuscript.
This gives you a versioned history. If someone asks “what affiliation did I list at submission?” six months later, you reload the v1 JSON via Import project (.json) and see exactly what was sent.


What if I switch computers or browsers between revisions?
localStorage is browser-specific. Before switching:

On the old browser: Export project (.json).
Move the JSON file (email, Dropbox, Drive, USB).
On the new browser: open ordenaR → Import project (.json).

Everything is restored — authors, affiliations, CRediT, sort order, equal-contribution groups, current journal format.


Does ordenaR remember which journal I last used?
Yes. The selected output format and sort mode are saved with the project, so reopening (or re-importing the JSON) brings you back exactly where you left off.

title: FAQ
text:
Compatibility

Which browsers are supported?
Any modern browser updated within the last 2 years: Chrome, Firefox, Safari, Edge. ordenaR uses no obscure web APIs — just standard Clipboard, Drag-and-Drop, FileReader, and localStorage. Mobile-friendly down to 375 px wide.


Does it work offline?
Yes, after the first load. The site is fully static; once your browser has cached index.html, app.js, and styles.css, you can disconnect from the internet and keep editing.

title: FAQ
text:
Outputs

Which journals are supported?
ordenaR exports 30+ formats that cover the dominant style families: generic plain text (Nature/Science, Elsevier), journal-specific plain text (PLOS, Cell Press, PNAS, eLife, MDPI, Wiley, Frontiers, Royal Society, Nature Communications, Nature Methods, AGU, APA, Vancouver), an Ecology & Evolution suite, LaTeX (elsarticle, Springer svjour3, IEEE), Quarto/RMarkdown YAML, Word HTML, BibTeX, CSL JSON, EndNote XML, a Markdown profiles table, and a CRediT statement listing each author’s contributor roles.


What if my journal isn’t supported?
Most journals follow one of the major publisher patterns (Elsevier, Wiley, Springer, Cell). Pick the closest match — the output is almost always copy-pasteable as-is. The plain-text Nature/Science format is also a safe universal fallback.


How is ORCID validated?
Using the official ISO 7064 MOD 11-2 checksum on the 16-digit identifier. The validation is local; we never query orcid.org.


How are duplicate affiliations deduplicated?
By exact-match normalized comparison of department + institution + city + country. Lowercasing, whitespace collapse, and trimming are applied before comparison.

title: FAQ
text:
Open source

What’s the license?
MIT for the code, CC-BY 4.0 for the documentation. See LICENSE and LICENSE-docs in the GitHub repo.


Where do I report a bug?
GitHub Issues: https://github.com/felixpleiva/ordenaR/issues.
